planting.note: Direct sow in spring in dry to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile to lean soil in full sun; bitterweed is a native annual of disturbed, open habitats throughout the Gulf Coastal Plain, self-sowing prolifically in roadsides, old fields, and open sandy or clay soils.

directions: Direct sow in dry to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; bitterweed is a native annual helenium that colonizes disturbed, open habitats throughout the Gulf South with cheerful yellow flowers with drooping ray petals around a prominent spherical yellow-green center disk — a distinctive and attractive flower form. Despite its common name and extreme bitterness (which makes it poisonous to livestock that eat it in large quantities), the plant is highly ornamental in naturalistic and wildflower garden settings. The bright flowers are excellent nectar sources for native bees, particularly small bees and sweat bees. The plant self-sows freely to form self-sustaining populations in appropriate open habitats. In zone 9a, it blooms from midsummer through fall, providing extended season color in dry, sunny areas. The bitterness deters deer and rabbit browsing.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for spring germination in moist to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; purple-headed sneezeweed grows naturally in moist, open habitats, roadside ditches, and moist meadows across the eastern and central US.

directions: Plant in moist to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; purple-headed sneezeweed is a distinctive native helenium recognizable by its dark maroon-purple disc (center of the flower head) — an unusual coloration that contrasts dramatically with the reflexed yellow ray petals. The flowers are produced abundantly from summer through fall on branched stems. Like all heleniums, the flowers are magnetically attractive to native bumblebees, sweat bees, and skippers and are important late-season nectar sources. The 'winged' stems — where leaf bases extend down the stem as flanges — are a characteristic helenium trait. Purple-headed sneezeweed tolerates partial shade better than the closely related H. autumnale and is adaptable to a wide range of moist garden soils. An underappreciated native for colorful late summer borders.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for spring germination in well-drained, dry, lean, slightly acidic to neutral or calcareous soil in full sun; fragrant gaillardia grows naturally in dry, rocky, calcareous, or sandy prairies and open woodlands of the south-central US from Texas to Kansas.

directions: Plant in dry, lean, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral or calcareous soil in full sun; fragrant gaillardia is the only pleasantly fragrant species in the blanket flower genus — most gaillardias are odorless, but G. suavis produces a sweet, clover-like fragrance from its rounded, ball-like flower heads, which lack the ray (petal) flowers typical of the genus and instead produce only disc flowers — giving it a uniquely spherical, pom-pom appearance. It is native to the dry prairies and open rocky soils of the south-central US and is exceptionally drought and heat tolerant. The fragrance makes it unusual and valuable in the sensory or cut-flower garden. An underused native for dry, sunny, prairie-style or xeriscape plantings in zone 9a where it requires minimal care once established.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for spring germination after natural cold stratification; rough rosinweed grows in dry, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, open prairie and savanna soils. Develops a deep taproot over years; best sown directly where it is to grow permanently.

directions: Plant in dry to moderately moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; rough rosinweed is a drought-tolerant native Silphium of open prairies, dry savannas, and open woodlands across the southeastern US, producing multiple-branched stems with numerous yellow daisy flowers in summer. The stiffly rough, sandpaper-textured leaves give the plant a tactile distinctiveness. Like all rosinweeds, it produces a resinous, aromatic sap and develops a deep taproot that makes established plants very drought tolerant and also nearly impossible to transplant. Direct sow is strongly preferred over transplanting for this species. The seeds are highly attractive to goldfinches and other finches in late summer and fall. A mid-to-tall native prairie plant providing excellent structural interest and wildlife food in naturalistic gardens.

planting.note: Direct sow fresh seeds in fall for spring germination; composite rosinweed is a Coastal Plain species growing in dry, sandy, acidic, open pine flatwoods and sandhills. One of the few rosinweeds adapted to the acidic, sandy soils of the southeastern Coastal Plain.

directions: Plant in dry, sandy, strongly acidic, infertile, well-drained soil in full sun; composite rosinweed is a southeastern Coastal Plain endemic adapted to the dry, sandy, acidic longleaf pine savanna ecosystem — one of the most challenging soil environments for plants. Unlike most Silphiums that prefer richer, more neutral soils, this species is particularly suited to the lean, sandy, infertile soils of pine flatwoods and sandhills. The deeply divided, almost fern-like leaves are ornamentally distinctive and unlike any other rosinweed. Tall branched stems carry numerous small yellow daisy flowers in summer. The seeds attract goldfinches and other seed-eaters. A valuable component of longleaf pine ecosystem restoration plantings and an unusual addition to dry, sandy, native plant gardens in zone 9a.
